network.overall_strategy: The network centers on the literature-supported conclusion that prebiotic isoleucine is most plausibly generated by Strecker chemistry from 2-methylbutanal, NH3, and HCN in volcanic/spark-discharge settings, with subsequent aqueous hydrolysis of the aminonitrile. To satisfy environmental interconnection, the network also includes hydrothermal generation of central carbon intermediates (formate, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, glyoxylate) that can feed surface aldehyde and alpha-keto-acid chemistry, plus biochemical/prebiotic conversion of alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate to isoleucine by reductive amination, Bucherer-Bergs-type chemistry, and transamination analogs. The design explicitly reflects the literature constraint that cyanosulfidic chemistry and standard hydrothermal vent simulations do not directly produce isoleucine, so those environments are used only for precursor supply or speculative-but-chemically-motivated keto-acid routes.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 on Fe-S phases yields formate as an early C1 fixation product.
reactions.reasoning: Fe-S minerals catalyze CO2 reduction under alkaline hydrothermal conditions and are central to iron-sulfur world carbon fixation chemistry; this supplies prebiotic C1 feedstock for downstream aldehyde and keto-acid networks (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Martin & Russell, 2007; Grosch et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type formation of acetaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: CO2/CO hydrogenation on metal-rich mineral surfaces produces reduced C2 carbonyl products including acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The literature report notes Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on metal surfaces as a plausible route to aldehydes relevant to Strecker chemistry and specifically mentions FTT as one possible source of 2-methylbutanal precursors (Pizzarello & Cronin, 2008; Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate formation from CO2 and H2
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated multicarbon fixation yields alpha-keto acid products such as pyruvate under reducing vent conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is an established central prebiotic metabolite in hydrothermal/protometabolic models and is explicitly highlighted in the supplied framework as a hydrothermal product feeding later chemistry; it also underpins the alpha-keto-acid route to isoleucine precursors (Springsteen et al., 2018; Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal generation of glyoxylate from central carbon chemistry
reactions.mechanism: Fe-S mediated oxidation-state redistribution within reduced C1/C2 carbon pools furnishes glyoxylate as a protometabolic alpha-oxoacid intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Glyoxylate is identified in the research summary as a central prebiotic metabolite relevant to alpha-ketobutyrate formation via nonenzymatic network logic, though a specific direct hydrothermal preparation remains underconstrained; this step is included as a connective but plausible protometabolic link grounded in glyoxylate-centered prebiotic metabolism (Springsteen et al., 2018; speculative connector explicitly required to connect literature-supported keto-acid routes).

reactions.name: Volcanic spark-discharge generation of HCN and NH3

conditions.other: volcanic atmosphere with CH4/NH3/CO2/H2S/H2O, electrical discharge, strong UV

reactions.mechanism: Energetic atmospheric processing forms HCN and related reduced nitrogen compounds that dissolve into surface waters.
reactions.reasoning: HCN and NH3 are core Strecker reagents generated in Miller-type discharge systems; Parker et al. showed H2S-containing volcanic discharge settings are uniquely productive for branched amino acids including isoleucine. Mineral listing is included to satisfy the required surface-agent schema, though the primary energy source here is discharge chemistry rather than heterogeneous catalysis (Miller, 1957; Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Surface formation of propanal from acetaldehyde under discharge/radical chemistry

conditions.other: volcanic spark/radical regime, H2S-enhanced branched-chain precursor formation

reactions.mechanism: Radical homologation and carbonyl-chain growth convert lower aldehydes into C3 aldehydes usable in crossed aldol chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The literature identifies radical chemistry during electrical discharge as the key source of branched aldehyde precursors and specifically cites crossed aldol chemistry from smaller aldehydes as a plausible route toward 2-methylbutanal. This step formalizes the smaller-aldehyde buildup needed for that route (Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Pizzarello & Cronin, 2008).

reactions.name: Surface crossed-aldol route to 2-methylbutanal

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling, surface concentration, aldehyde condensation and reduction sequence

reactions.mechanism: Crossed aldol condensation between lower aldehydes followed by dehydration/reduction furnishes the branched C5 aldehyde 2-methylbutanal.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied research explicitly identifies crossed aldol condensation of acetaldehyde with propanal as one plausible prebiotic route to 2-methylbutanal, the critical Strecker precursor for isoleucine. This is a necessary branching-carbon construction step, though the full nonenzymatic sequence remains incompletely demonstrated experimentally (Pizzarello & Cronin, 2008; speculative-but-literature-grounded connection).

reactions.name: Surface direct radical formation of 2-methylbutanal in H2S-rich discharge chemistry

conditions.other: volcanic spark discharge with H2S, radical recombination of alkyl fragments

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge generates alkyl radicals whose recombination and oxidation yield branched aldehydes including 2-methylbutanal.
reactions.reasoning: The strongest evidence for isoleucine synthesis comes from H2S-containing Miller discharge experiments, where sulfur radical chemistry promoted branched-chain amino acid production. The research specifically identifies radical recombination during discharges as a plausible source of 2-methylbutanal (Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Pizzarello & Cronin, 2008).

reactions.name: Surface Strecker addition to isoleucine aminonitrile

conditions.other: aqueous Strecker conditions after atmospheric discharge products dissolve into pools

reactions.mechanism: 2-Methylbutanal condenses with NH3 to an imine, followed by HCN addition to form the alpha-aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the dominant and best-supported prebiotic route to isoleucine. The literature explicitly states that isoleucine formation proceeds through Strecker chemistry from 2-methylbutanal, HCN, and ammonia in Miller-type systems, with aminonitrile as the key intermediate (Miller, 1957; Ring et al., 1972; Eckhardt et al., 2022; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Biochemical/prebiotic hydrolysis of isoleucine aminonitrile to isoleucine

conditions.other: prolonged hydrolysis in prebiotic pools; racemic isoleucine/alloisoleucine expected

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile hydrolysis through amide/carboxylate stages produces the alpha-amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of the Strecker aminonitrile to isoleucine is the terminal step of the canonical prebiotic Strecker pathway and underlies the isoleucine detections in spark-discharge experiments and meteorite-parent-body chemistry (Miller, 1957; Ring et al., 1972; Pizzarello & Cronin, 2008).

reactions.name: Biochemical/protometabolic formation of alpha-ketobutyrate from glyoxylate and pyruvate

conditions.other: nonenzymatic protometabolic alpha-keto-acid network in pools/mineral interfaces

reactions.mechanism: Carbon-carbon bond formation within alpha-keto-acid chemistry extends pyruvate/glyoxylate carbon skeletons to alpha-ketobutyrate.
reactions.reasoning: The research highlights alpha-ketobutyrate as a biologically central branch-point and notes that noncanonical routes from glyoxylate plus pyruvate exist in modern metabolism, which is prebiotically significant because both metabolites are plausible prebiotic intermediates. This exact prebiotic step remains unproven but is directly motivated by the supplied literature (Kim et al., 2020; Springsteen et al., 2018; speculative connector acknowledged).

reactions.name: Biochemical/protometabolic condensation of alpha-ketobutyrate to alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate

conditions.other: alpha-keto-acid condensation in protometabolic network; unresolved nonenzymatic analogue of BCAA pathway

reactions.mechanism: An acetyl/pyruvate-derived carbon unit is added to alpha-ketobutyrate to generate the C6 branched alpha-keto acid precursor of isoleucine.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied report states that alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate is the penultimate biological precursor and may be accessible by nonenzymatic aldol/alpha-keto-acid chemistry from alpha-ketobutyrate plus an acetyl equivalent, but this has not yet been demonstrated. It is included because reductive amination and Bucherer-Bergs routes to isoleucine depend on this precursor (Ter-Ovanessian et al., 2025; supplied report conclusion; explicitly speculative but necessary).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal FeS-mediated reductive amination of alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate to isoleucine

conditions.other: anaerobic acidic-to-neutral primordial amino acid synthesis conditions

reactions.mechanism: The alpha-keto acid undergoes imine formation with ammonia followed by mineral-assisted reduction to the alpha-amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-oxo acids with FeS was demonstrated for primordial amino acid synthesis and later revisited with freshly precipitated FeS or Fe(OH)2. The general chemistry is applicable to isoleucine if alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate is available, but the precursor's prebiotic supply remains unresolved (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Huber & Wachtershauser, 2003).

reactions.name: Surface UV-photocatalytic amination of alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate to isoleucine

conditions.other: shallow sunlit pools; analogue to ZnS photocatalytic amination

reactions.mechanism: Photoexcited mineral surfaces drive reductive amination of the alpha-keto acid to the amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Wang et al. demonstrated reversible photocatalytic amination of alpha-keto acids on ZnS under UV illumination. The chemistry is general in principle and therefore extends to alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate, although direct isoleucine-specific demonstration has not been reported. This is a literature-based extrapolation required to connect the known keto-acid route (Wang et al., 2012; supplied report explicitly discusses this application).

reactions.name: Biochemical Bucherer-Bergs conversion of alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate to hydantoin intermediate
reactions.mechanism: The alpha-keto acid reacts with cyanide and ammonia to form a hydantoin intermediate via Bucherer-Bergs-type chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. demonstrated that alpha-keto acids react with cyanide and ammonia through hydantoin intermediates to yield alpha-amino acids, bridging Strecker and metabolic chemistry. The report states this pathway is general and should apply to isoleucine's keto-acid precursor if present (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of isoleucine hydantoin to isoleucine
reactions.mechanism: Ring opening and hydrolysis of the hydantoin intermediate produce the alpha-amino acid isoleucine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the terminal step of Bucherer-Bergs chemistry. While direct isoleucine-specific experimental demonstration is not cited in the report, the reaction is presented as a general route for alpha-keto acids and therefore a justified extension to alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; speculative extension noted).

reactions.name: Biochemical transamination analogue from alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate to isoleucine

conditions.other: mineral-interface or pool chemistry, reductant available

reactions.mechanism: Amine transfer or reductive transamination analogue converts the alpha-keto acid directly to isoleucine.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied report treats reductive amination/transamination-style conversion of alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate as the direct metabolic analogue to isoleucine biosynthesis. Nonenzymatic realization is less well established than FeS-mediated amination, so this is included as a weakly supported connector rather than a primary route (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Huber & Wachtershauser, 2003; supplied report conclusion).

pathways.name: Canonical spark-Strecker isoleucine pathway
pathways.description: Volcanic discharge produces HCN/NH3 and branched aldehydes; 2-methylbutanal undergoes Strecker synthesis to aminonitrile, then hydrolysis to isoleucine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde to crossed-aldol Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal FTT chemistry generates acetaldehyde, which is homologated to propanal and converted to 2-methylbutanal by surface aldol chemistry before Strecker synthesis.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde to radical-discharge Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde feeds H2S-rich volcanic discharge chemistry that directly forms 2-methylbutanal, followed by Strecker conversion.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-central-metabolism to FeS reductive amination pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate and glyoxylate feed a protometabolic alpha-keto-acid network to alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate, then FeS-mediated reductive amination yields isoleucine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-central-metabolism to UV amination pathway
pathways.description: Alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate produced from pyruvate/glyoxylate is aminated in sunlit surface pools by photocatalytic sulfide-mineral chemistry.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-central-metabolism to Bucherer-Bergs pathway
pathways.description: Protometabolic alpha-keto-acid construction furnishes alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate, which reacts with cyanide and ammonia via hydantoin to isoleucine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-central-metabolism to transamination-analogue pathway
pathways.description: Alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate formed in protometabolic chemistry is directly converted to isoleucine by a biochemical/prebiotic amination analogue.

pathways.name: Formate-assisted hydrothermal carbon feed to Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal C1 fixation supplies reduced carbon pool through formate and acetaldehyde generation, eventually feeding surface aldehyde buildup and Strecker synthesis.

pathways.name: Mixed discharge and aldol route to Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Atmospheric discharge supplies HCN/NH3 while hydrothermal acetaldehyde and surface propanal chemistry converge at 2-methylbutanal before Strecker addition.

pathways.name: Fully interconnected hydrothermal-surface-biochemical route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon fixation and surface discharge chemistry jointly supply both the Strecker branch and the alpha-keto-acid branch, converging on isoleucine through alternative late-stage chemistries.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest pathway in the network and aligns best with the literature: spark/discharge generation of HCN/NH3, formation of branched aldehyde precursor, Strecker aminonitrile formation, and hydrolysis to isoleucine. The main weakness is that direct generation of both HCN and NH3 from CO2 alone is chemically underspecified, and the direct radical route to 2-methylbutanal is plausible but not strongly constrained without explicit reduced carbon feedstocks such as CH4/H2S-containing mixtures.
suggestions: Specify reduced atmospheric inputs for discharge chemistry rather than CO2 alone
Explicitly include H2S and/or CH4 in the aldehyde-forming discharge environment
Clarify that Strecker produces racemic isoleucine/alloisoleucine mixtures

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is connected and reaches the target, but several early steps are weak. Hydrothermal direct formation of acetaldehyde from CO2 and H2 is plausible only in broad FTT terms and usually gives mixtures rather than a clean product. Surface conversion of acetaldehyde to propanal and then crossed-aldol access to 2-methylbutanal is chemically conceivable, but the exact sequence is not well supported experimentally for prebiotic isoleucine synthesis.
suggestions: Replace direct acetaldehyde-to-propanal with a better-supported homologation sequence
Document mineral catalysts and discharge composition for the aldehyde-building steps
Treat the crossed-aldol route as speculative unless literature support is added

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route is somewhat better than the crossed-aldol branch because radical discharge chemistry for branched aldehydes is literature-consistent. However, the hydrothermal acetaldehyde entry point remains weak, and the path still relies on an underspecified propanal-forming step. The final Strecker-hydrolysis segment is strong.
suggestions: Make the discharge environment the primary source of both propanal and 2-methylbutanal
Avoid implying strong support for hydrothermal acetaldehyde as a clean precursor supply
Include explicit H2S-assisted radical chemistry

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This metabolism-first route is the weakest major route. While pyruvate and glyoxylate are plausible prebiotic central metabolites, the conversion to alpha-ketobutyrate is only loosely inspired by protometabolic chemistry, the construction of alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate is highly speculative, and direct FeS-mediated reductive amination to isoleucine in hydrothermal conditions conflicts with the literature warning that hydrothermal vent simulations have not produced isoleucine. In addition, high-temperature hydrothermal settings would threaten amino acid stability.
suggestions: Downgrade this entire route to speculative status
Use mild alkaline vent or shallow hydrothermal conditions if retaining reductive amination
Add a missing reduction/isomerization/dehydration sequence between keto-acid condensation and amino acid formation
Avoid claiming direct hydrothermal production of isoleucine from alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate without supporting evidence

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The UV amination endpoint is not well aligned with the cited literature because ZnS, not TiO2, is the key photocatalyst reported for reversible alpha-keto-acid amination. The upstream assembly of alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate is also speculative. Overall this route is conceptually interesting but poorly supported in its current implementation.
suggestions: Substitute ZnS for TiO2 if photoreductive amination is intended
Add literature-backed support for alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate formation or mark it explicitly as hypothetical
Clarify whether this is shallow-surface photochemistry rather than generic surface UV chemistry

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The Bucherer-Bergs branch is more literature-aligned than the direct amination routes because hydantoin-mediated conversion of alpha-keto acids to amino acids is experimentally supported. However, the bottleneck remains the unsupported prebiotic availability of alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate. Thus the endpoint chemistry is reasonable, but the pathway as a whole is not yet convincing.
suggestions: Retain Bucherer-Bergs as a conditional branch contingent on alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate availability
Explicitly note generality extrapolation from pyruvate-to-alanine literature
Improve the precursor-forming keto-acid network

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the least supported endpoint. True transamination requires a coupled amino donor/acceptor system and is not captured by simply adding ammonia to an alpha-keto acid. As a prebiotic analogue this is too underspecified, and the upstream keto-acid route remains speculative.
suggestions: Remove or heavily qualify the transamination-analogue route
Include an actual amino donor and plausible redox coupling if retaining this chemistry
Focus on Strecker or Bucherer-Bergs branches instead

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Formate is a credible hydrothermal hub, but its role here does not convincingly strengthen the route to isoleucine because the network does not specify how formate materially feeds branched aldehyde synthesis. The final Strecker segment is strong; the integration of formate into that branch is comparatively weak.
suggestions: Show explicit carbon flow from formate into aldehyde homologation or remove the 'formate-assisted' label
Provide a more chemically justified link between hydrothermal C1 chemistry and C5 branched aldehyde assembly

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This mixed discharge/aldol route is acceptable and reaches the target coherently. It is weaker than the canonical spark-Strecker route because the aldehyde elaboration steps are less directly supported than simple discharge-derived branched aldehyde generation. Still, it is internally consistent.
suggestions: Provide stronger literature support for aldol access to 2-methylbutanal under prebiotic conditions
Specify minerals for the surface aldol chemistry
Clarify atmospheric composition in the discharge step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway best showcases full network interconnection across environments, but it also inherits several weak or speculative steps from both the hydrothermal precursor branch and the keto-acid branch. As a systems narrative it is strong; as a chemically rigorous path to isoleucine it is only moderate.
suggestions: Use P1 as the primary route and keep hydrothermal branches as precursor-supply context only
Reduce reliance on speculative keto-acid assembly steps
Tighten mass-flow logic between interconnected subroutes

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically strong and clearly connected, with adequate node counts, multiple hubs, and several convergent routes. It succeeds particularly well in identifying the literature-supported core of prebiotic isoleucine synthesis: spark/discharge generation of branched aldehyde precursors followed by Strecker chemistry and aminonitrile hydrolysis. Environmental interconnection is also present, with hydrothermal carbon feedstocks linked into surface chemistry and surface products feeding biochemical assembly. However, the network falls short chemically and in literature alignment in several important places. First, multiple reactions are underspecified or implausible as written: CO2 alone as source of both HCN and NH3 in discharge chemistry, hydrothermal clean synthesis of acetaldehyde or pyruvate from CO2/H2, glyoxylate generation from formate, and especially direct assembly of alpha-keto-beta-methylvalerate. Second, several endpoint routes overstate support: hydrothermal vent chemistry has not been shown to produce isoleucine, TiO2 is not the best-supported photocatalyst for reductive amination of alpha-keto acids, and the transamination analogue is too vague to count as a valid prebiotic transformation. Third, the summary omits the requirement that hydrothermal and surface reactions specify mineral agents, which is a formal deficiency under the synthesis-network specification. Literature alignment is mixed: the canonical Strecker branch is well aligned, Bucherer-Bergs is reasonably extrapolated, but the metabolism-first keto-acid routes remain speculative and should be marked more explicitly as such. Overall, this is a good but not approvable network: strong topology and a correct main strategy, but too many chemically weak auxiliary reactions to justify very high confidence.
